column4: Clause Text
column5: New Question
column6: Yes Logic/Result
column8: Guidance

column2: The property is a listed building. Listed Building Consent required for any works. Permitted Development does not apply.
column3: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990, ss.7 & 9
column4: Any works affecting a listed building require Listed Building Consent; PD rights do not apply.
column5: Is your property a listed building, officially recognised for its special architectural or historic interest?
column6: If Yes ? your project will need special consent and cannot rely on standard permitted development rights. If No ? you can continue with normal planning rules.
column8: Permitted Development rights do not apply to listed buildings. Any works will require Listed Building Consent and possibly planning permission, regardless of scale or type.

column2: The property is in a conservation area. Demolition and many extensions require planning permission; check for Article 4 directions. LDC can clarify.
column3: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990, ss.69 & 72
column4: Works affecting conservation areas may require planning permission; PD rights are restricted.
column5: Is your property located in a conservation area, which is a special area recognised for its character or history?
column6: If Yes ? some works may need full planning permission, and additional checks may apply. If No ? standard planning rules apply.
column8: Permitted Development rights are more limited in conservation areas. Some works may still be allowed, but additional restrictions apply. A Lawful Development Certificate can confirm whether PD applies.

column2: The property is covered by an Article 4 Direction. Some or all Permitted Development rights withdrawn; usually affects extensions, outbuildings, HMOs. LDC can clarify.
column4: Local authorities can withdraw specific PD rights in defined areas; works that would normally be PD may require permission.
column5: Has your property been specifically restricted by your local council so that some standard planning rights no longer apply? (Article 4)
column6: If Yes ? some types of work that are normally allowed will require planning permission. If No ? you can continue with normal permitted development rules.
column8: An Article 4 Direction removes specific Permitted Development rights. Even minor works may need planning permission. Check the scope of the direction or confirm via an Lawful Development Certficate (LDC).

column2: The property is on designated land (AONB, National Park, WHS). Additional limits apply to extensions, outbuildings, roof works. LDC can clarify.
column4: PD rights are restricted on designated land (National Parks, AONB, WHS); extra conditions apply.
column5: Is your property located within a protected area, such as a National Park, Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, or World Heritage Site?
column6: If Yes ? extra limits apply and some work may require full planning permission. If No ? normal planning rules apply.
column8: Permitted Development rights are more restricted on designated land. Some development may still be possible, but tighter limits apply and planning permission is more likely. An Lawful Development Certficate (LDC) can help confirm.

column2: The project is a completely new dwelling. Full planning permission likely required; optional Outline ? Reserved Matters.
column3: GPDO 2015 does not permit new dwellings
column4: New dwellings are not PD; full planning permission required.
column5: Are you planning to build a completely new house on this site?
column6: If Yes ? full planning permission likely required.
column8: Permitted Development rights do not apply to new dwellings. Full planning permission will be required.

column2: The project replaces an existing dwelling. Planning permission required if footprint/layout changes.
column3: GPDO 2015 does not permit replacement dwellings with footprint changes
column4: PD does not allow replacement if footprint changes; planning permission required.
column5: Are you planning to replace an existing house with a new one?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required if footprint/layout changes.
column8: Permitted Development rights do not apply if footprint or layout changes. Planning permission required.

column2: The project includes extensions or additions to the property.
column3: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 1, Classes A, AA
column4: Extensions and alterations may be PD subject to size limits, conditions, and exceptions.
column5: Will you be adding extensions or additional buildings to the existing property?
column6: If Yes ? Permitted Development may apply, some checks needed.

column2: The project involves converting or changing the use of the property.
column4: Certain change-of-use classes (e.g., commercial-to-residential, dwelling to HMO) may be PD, subject to conditions and limits.
column5: Will you be converting the property or changing its use (e.g., residential to commercial or flats)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission may be needed, check PD rules.

column2: The project involves demolishing a building or doing other site works (fences, walls, gates, driveways).
column4: Minor works (fences, walls, gates) may be PD; demolition often requires planning permission and may be restricted in conservation areas.
column5: Will your project involve demolishing an existing building or carrying out site works like fences, walls, gates, or driveways?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission may be required; check building control.

column2: The project includes a loft extension. Permitted Development may apply if within limits; Building Control required. Consider LDC to confirm.
column4: Enlargement, improvement, or alteration of dwellinghouse; max 40m│ in conservation areas, 50m│ elsewhere.
column5: Are you planning a loft extension?
column6: If Yes ? PD may apply, building control needed.

column2: The project includes a single-storey extension. Permitted Development may apply; Building Control required. Consider LDC to confirm.
column4: Single-storey extension; max 4m for detached, 3m for others; max 50% of curtilage coverage; PD conditions apply.
column5: Are you planning a single-storey extension?
column6: If Yes ? PD may apply, building control needed.

column2: The project includes a multi-storey extension. Planning permission required; Building Control required.
column3: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 1, Class A / AA
column4: Multi-storey extensions often require permission unless within strict size/height limits; PD restrictions apply.
column5: Are you planning a multi-storey extension?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required, building control needed.
column8: Multi-storey extensions usually cannot be done under permitted development. You will need full planning permission, and Building Control approval is required to ensure the structure is safe. Professional advice is strongly recommended before starting.

column2: The project includes a multi-storey extension. Planning permission required; Building Control required.
column4: Conservatories treated as single-storey extensions; size limits and PD conditions apply.
column5: Are you planning a conservatory or sunroom?
column6: If Yes ? PD may apply, building control needed.

column2: The project includes a garage or conversion of a garage. Permitted Development may apply; Building Control required if habitable.
column4: Outbuildings incidental to dwelling; max 50% of curtilage; height limits apply; PD conditions.
column5: Are you planning a new garage or converting an existing garage?
column6: If Yes ? PD may apply; building control required if habitable.

column2: The project includes an external building (workshop, garden office, pool building). Permitted Development may apply; Building Control required if serviced/habitable. Consider LDC to confirm.
column4: Outbuildings incidental to dwelling; size, height, and location limits; PD conditions apply.
column5: Are you planning any external building like a workshop, garden office, or pool building?
column6: If Yes ? PD may apply; building control required if serviced/habitable.

column2: The project includes a balcony. Planning permission required; Building Control required for structural load.
column3: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 1, Class A (roof alterations may include balconies)
column4: Balconies usually not PD; planning permission required; PD only applies for minor roof alterations in limited cases.
column5: Are you planning to add a balcony?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission and building control required.
column8: Adding a balcony is generally not allowed under permitted development. Planning permission is required, and Building Control approval is needed for structural safety. Seek professional advice to confirm requirements and options.

column2: The project includes a porch. Permitted Development may apply if small; Building Control usually required.
column4: Porch permitted up to 3m high and 3m from original dwelling; PD conditions apply.
column5: Are you planning to add a porch?
column6: If Yes ? PD may apply if small; building control usually required.

column2: The project includes an annex or granny flat. Permitted Development may apply if ancillary; Building Control required.
column4: Ancillary buildings; PD conditions include single-storey, max height, curtilage limits, not in front of principal elevation.
column5: Are you planning an annex or granny flat?
column6: If Yes ? PD may apply if ancillary; building control required.

column2: The project converts a single dwelling into flats or bedsits. Planning permission required; Building Control required.
column4: Change of use from dwelling to multiple dwellings may be permitted in some areas; conditions apply.
column5: Are you converting a single dwelling into flats or bedsits?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission and Building Control required.
column8: Planning permission is required. You cannot use permitted rights. Building Control approval is also required before starting work. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project converts flats into a single dwelling. May be Permitted Development; Building Control required.
column4: Converting multiple dwellings into one may be permitted subject to conditions.
column5: Are you converting flats into a single dwelling?
column6: If Yes ? may be allowed; Building Control required.

column2: The project converts a single dwelling into a House in Multiple Occupation (HMO). Planning permission required, check licensing and fire safety.
column4: Some HMO conversions may be permitted; local licensing/fire safety conditions apply.
column5: Are you converting a single dwelling into a House in Multiple Occupation (HMO)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; check local housing and safety rules.
column8: Planning permission is required. You must check any local rules for housing or safety. Building Control may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project converts a commercial building into residential. Permitted Development possible under Class MA; Building Control required. Consider LDC to confirm.
column4: Permitted development allowed from office to residential under Class MA; conditions include size, prior approval, and limits.
column5: Are you converting a commercial building into residential?
column6: If Yes ? submit prior approval application; Building Control required.
column8: You may be able to convert this property under Permitted Development rights. Submit a prior approval application to your council Ś this checks whether specific impacts are acceptable. You can also request a Lawful Development Certificate, which confirms the conversion is allowed. Building Control approval may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project converts space above shops into residential. Permitted Development may apply; Building Control required.
column4: Change of use from commercial/retail to residential may be permitted under Class O; prior approval required.
column5: Are you converting space above shops into residential?
column6: If Yes ? submit prior approval application; Building Control required.
column8: You may be able to convert the space above a shop under Permitted Development rights. Submit a prior approval application to your council to confirm the conversion is acceptable. You can also request a Lawful Development Certificate confirming the conversion is allowed. Building Control approval may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project converts a residential building into commercial. Planning permission required; Building Control required.
column4: Residential to commercial change generally requires planning permission; permitted development exceptions limited.
column5: Are you converting a residential building into commercial use?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission and Building Control required.
column8: Planning permission is required. You cannot use permitted rights. Building Control approval is also required. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project is a general change of use. Some changes may be Permitted Development; Building Control may apply.
column4: Some changes of use may be permitted; prior approval and conditions apply.
column5: Are you making a general change of use to the property?
column6: If Yes ? submit prior approval application; Building Control may be required.
column8: Some general changes of use may be allowed without full planning permission. Submit a prior approval application to your council to confirm. You can also request a Lawful Development Certificate confirming the change is allowed. Building Control may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project involves demolishing a building. Often requires planning (especially in conservation areas); Building Control required.
column3: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 11 / local planning rules
column4: Demolition often requires permission; PD does not generally cover demolition in conservation areas.
column5: Will your project involve demolishing a building?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission often required; building control required.
column8: Planning permission is often required, particularly in conservation areas. Building Control approval is also required. Professional advice is recommended to ensure all requirements are met.

column2: The project involves other site works (fences, walls, gates, driveways). Some works permitted; check height/SuDS; Building Control rarely applies.
column3: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 2, Classes A & F
column4: Minor operations (fences, walls, gates) and hard surfaces may be PD; limits on height, location, and materials apply.
column5: Will your project involve other site works like fences, walls, gates, or driveways?
column6: If Yes ? some works may be permitted; check height/SuDS; building control rarely applies.
column8: Some minor site works may be allowed without full planning permission. You should check local rules on height, location, and materials. Building Control rarely applies. Professional advice is recommended if unsure.

column2: The site is in a flood risk area. Flood Risk Assessment may be required; affects Building Control design.
column4: PD rights may be limited in flood risk areas; Building Control requirements may change.
column5: Is your site in a flood risk area?
column6: If Yes ? flood risk assessment may be required; affects building control.
column8: If your site is in a flood risk area, a Flood Risk Assessment may be required to comply with regulations and ensure safe design. Building Control requirements may change. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project affects highway access, parking, or visibility. Transport assessment or highways approval may be required.
column4: PD rights may be restricted if highway safety impacted; approval required.
column5: Will your project affect highway access, parking, or visibility?
column6: If Yes ? transport assessment or highways approval may be required.
column8: If your project affects access, parking, or visibility, a transport assessment or highways approval may be needed to protect safety. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The site is potentially contaminated. Site investigation or remediation may be required.
column4: PD rights may be restricted on contaminated sites; investigation/remediation may be required.
column5: Is your site potentially contaminated?
column6: If Yes ? site investigation or remediation may be required.
column8: If your site may be contaminated, a site investigation or remediation may be required to make it safe. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The project affects protected habitats, ecology, or trees. Ecological survey or Arboricultural Impact Assessment may be required.
column3: Wildlife & Countryside Act 1981 / Tree Preservation Orders
column4: PD rights limited if protected species, habitats, or trees affected; surveys may be required.
column5: Will your project affect protected habitats, ecology, or trees?
column6: If Yes ? ecological survey or arboricultural assessment may be required.
column8: If your project affects habitats, protected species, or trees, an ecological survey or arboricultural assessment may be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column2: The site is in an area of archaeological interest. Desk-based or on-site archaeological assessment may be required.
column4: PD rights restricted if archaeological interest present; assessment may be required.
column5: Is your site in an area of archaeological interest?
column6: If Yes ? desk-based or on-site archaeological assessment may be required.
column8: If your site is in an area of archaeological interest, a desk-based or on-site archaeological assessment may be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Certain roof types may remove PD eligibility
column5: Is the loft a Mansard roof (a roof with two slopes on each side, the lower one being steeper)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Mansard roofs are not allowed under permitted development rights. Planning permission is required, and professional advice is recommended to confirm requirements.

column4: Increasing eaves above original height may remove PD
column5: Does the loft raise the height of the walls at the edge of the roof (eaves) above the original?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Raising the eaves above the original height removes permitted development rights. Planning permission is required, and professional advice is recommended.

column4: Volume limits: 40m│ in conservation, 50m│ elsewhere
column5: Will the total new space created by the loft exceed 40m│ in a conservation area or 50m│ elsewhere?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? generally allowed, building control required
column8: If the total new loft space exceeds 40m│ in a conservation area or 50m│ elsewhere ? planning permission is required. If it is under these limits ? generally allowed, but Building Control approval may still be needed. Professional advice is recommended if unsure or in a conservation area.

column3: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column4: Works in conservation areas or listed buildings are restricted
column5: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building (protected for historic or architectural interest)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission / LDC required; If No ? continue
column8: If the property is in a conservation area or is listed, permitted development rights do not apply. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Projection limits differ by property type
column5: Will the extension project more than 4m if detached, or 3m if semi/terraced?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If the extension projects more than 4m (detached) or 3m (semi/terraced), planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended to confirm limits.

column4: Height limit: 4m for single-storey
column5: Will the extension exceed 4m in height?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Single-storey extensions over 4m high are not allowed under permitted development rights. Planning permission is required, and professional advice is recommended.

column4: Total extension must not cover more than 50% of curtilage
column5: Will the total area of extensions and outbuildings exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If the total area of extensions and outbuildings exceeds 50% of the curtilage, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if unsure.

column4: Must be to rear; not in front of main elevation
column5: Is the extension at the rear of the property and not in front of the main elevation?
column6: If No ? planning permission required; If Yes ? continue
column8: Extensions must be to the rear of the property. Front extensions require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended.

column3: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column4: Extensions in conservation areas or on listed buildings are restricted
column5: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building (protected for historic or architectural interest)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission / LDC required; If No ? continue
column8: Properties in conservation areas or listed buildings cannot rely on permitted development rights. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Max projection: 4m for detached, 3m for others
column5: Will the conservatory project more than 4m if detached, or 3m if semi/terraced?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If the conservatory projects more than the allowed distance, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended to confirm measurements.

column4: Height limit: 4m (single-storey)
column5: Will the conservatory exceed 4m in height?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Conservatories over 4 metres in height require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended to confirm height measurements.

column4: Total area of extensions/outbuildings must not exceed 50% of curtilage
column5: Will the total area of the conservatory plus other extensions/outbuildings exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If extensions and outbuildings cover more than half of the original garden area, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if boundaries or previous works are unclear.

column4: Must be at least 2m from side boundaries if height >2.5m
column5: Is any part of the conservatory within 2m of the side boundary and taller than 2.5m?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If the conservatory is close to a boundary and exceeds the height limit, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended where boundary positions are uncertain.

column4: Habitable or serviced outbuildings may require building control
column5: Will the garage be converted into a habitable space (bedroom, office, etc.) or serviced (plumbing, kitchen, etc.)?
column6: If Yes ? building control required; planning rules depend on other limits
column8: Converting a garage into a habitable or serviced space normally requires Building Control approval to ensure safety and energy standards are met. Planning permission may still not be required if all other limits are met. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Height limit: 4m (3m if within 2m of boundary)
column5: Will the garage exceed 4m in height (or 3m if within 2m of boundary)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If the garage exceeds the allowed height, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended where measurements or boundary positions are unclear.

column4: Total outbuildings must not cover more than 50% of curtilage
column5: Will the total area of the garage plus other outbuildings/extensions exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If outbuildings and extensions cover more than half of the original garden area, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if previous works are uncertain.

column4: Must remain incidental to dwelling
column5: Will the garage be used as a separate dwelling or business?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Using a garage as a separate home or business is not automatically allowed and planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended to confirm acceptable use.

column4: Habitable or serviced buildings require building control
column5: Will the building be habitable or include services (plumbing, kitchen, heating, electrics)?
column6: If Yes ? building control required; planning rules depend on other limits
column8: If the building will be used as a habitable space or includes services, Building Control approval is normally required to ensure safety and energy standards are met. Planning permission may still not be required if all other limits are met. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Max height: 4m (3m if within 2m of boundary)
column5: Will the building exceed 4m in height (or 3m if within 2m of a boundary)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Buildings that exceed the height limits require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended where height measurements or boundary distances are unclear.

column4: Total outbuildings must not cover more than 50% of curtilage
column5: Will the total area of this building plus other extensions/outbuildings exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If outbuildings and extensions cover more than half of the original garden area, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if previous development is unclear.

column4: Must not be in front of the house; maintain distance from boundaries
column5: Is the building located at the rear or side of the house and at least 2m from any side boundary if taller than 2.5m?
column6: If No ? planning permission required; If Yes ? continue
column8: Buildings located in front of the house or too close to boundaries require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended where site layout or boundaries are uncertain.

column4: Max projection: 3m
column5: Will the porch project more than 3m from the original wall?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If the porch projects more than 3 metres from the original wall, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if measurements are unclear.

column4: Max height: 3m
column5: Will the porch exceed 3m in height?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Porches taller than 3 metres require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended to confirm height.

column4: Must not cover more than 50% of the frontage
column5: Will the porch cover more than 50% of the frontage of the house?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: If the porch would cover more than half of the frontage of the house, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if frontage boundaries are unclear.

column4: Must be ancillary to main dwelling
column5: Will the building be used as a separate dwelling or business?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: The annex must not be used as a separate dwelling or business. Professional advice is recommended if intended use is unclear.

column4: PD applies only for single-storey
column5: Will the annex be more than one storey?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Annexes taller than one storey require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended if plans are ambiguous.

column4: Maximum height: 4 metres, 3 metres if within 2 metres of boundary
column5: Will the annex exceed 4 metres in height (or 3 metres if within 2 metres of a boundary)?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Buildings exceeding these heights require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended for exact measurement.

column4: Must not cover more than 50% of the garden/curtilage
column5: Will the total area of the annex plus other outbuildings/extensions exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Annex plus other outbuildings must not exceed half of the garden. Professional advice recommended if boundaries are unclear.

column4: Must be at rear or side of main dwelling
column5: Is the annex located at the front of the main dwelling?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Annexes in front of the main dwelling are not PD. Professional advice is recommended if site layout is unclear.

column1: Question Set: Flat Conversion ¢ Limits and Checks (PS11)

column2: Converting flats into a single dwelling
column4: The use of a building as a single dwellinghouse instead of multiple flats can be permitted
column5: Are you converting more than one flat into a single home?
column6: If Yes ? the change of use is permitted development; Building Control approval is required; If No ? continue
column8: Converting more than one flat into a single home is allowed as permitted development, provided no other works require planning permission. Building Control approval is always required, and professional advice is recommended to confirm compliance.

column4: Height restrictions apply to extensions
column5: Will any extension exceed 4 metres in height, or 3 metres if it is within 2 metres of a boundary?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Extensions that exceed these height limits are not allowed under permitted development. Professional advice is recommended if measurements are unclear.

column4: Extensions must not cover more than half of the garden
column5: Will extensions and outbuildings cover more than 50 percent of the garden or curtilage?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: The total area of extensions and outbuildings must not cover more than half of the garden. Professional advice is recommended if boundaries are unclear.

column4: Extensions are limited by number of storeys
column5: Will any extension be more than one storey?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission required; If No ? continue
column8: Extensions of more than one storey are not allowed under permitted development. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: External changes may require planning permission
column5: Will the conversion involve external changes such as new windows, doors, extensions, or changes to the roof?
column6: If Yes ? planning permission may be required; If No ? continue
column8: External changes are likely to require planning permission if they alter the appearance of the building, add new openings, extend the building, or affect the front or roof. Professional advice is recommended before making any external changes.

column4: Structural alterations require Building Control approval
column5: Does the conversion involve structural work?
column6: If Yes ? Building Control approval required; If No ? continue
column8: Any structural changes must meet Building Control requirements. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Fire safety and access standards must be met
column5: Will the conversion affect fire safety or access, for example where the building has more than one storey?
column6: If Yes ? Building Control approval or professional advice required; If No ? continue
column8: Fire safety and safe access must be addressed as part of the conversion. Building Control approval is required, and professional advice is strongly recommended.
